part: Roots in Land, Church, and Fear
title: He Wanted All Ten of Us in Heaven
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Austin, Texas, on May 26, 2011, James Oscar Jones opens with his parents, Ernest and Curtis Jones of rural Arkansas, a father who raised all ten children (five from the mother's first marriage after that husband was killed cutting logs) on two goals: that all ten be educated and that all ten reach heaven, through church, hard work, and never missing a day of school.

title: He Bought His First Acre at Thirteen
summary: Jones describes the four-o'clock mornings milking cows before the bus, his father buying his first acre at age thirteen for a dollar and a quarter, adding to land from the McKinney side, and farming cotton and corn funded by cash from peach orchards and produce because the Farmers Home Administration would not lend to him.

title: A Few Bucks to Go to the Fair
summary: He recounts how his father gathered school dropouts who wandered after being kept out for farm work, paid them at the end of the week, and gave late-evening work so they would have money for the fair, escaping the plantation debt system where laborers were put on the books and never earned enough to pay off.

title: Everybody Knew Her as Vic
summary: Jones tells how his mother, formally Curtis Jones but known everywhere as Vic, drew hundreds to her funeral, attended NAACP meetings in Magnolia, and brought back to church people the message that they must improve their own homes and stop depending on outsiders, loading neighbors into a pickup truck without asking permission.

title: Stand Still at Twelve Noon
topic: The Brown Decision on the Radio
summary: He remembers his father's powerful radio that pulled in St. Louis baseball, Thurgood Marshall asking every Negro to stand still at noon for the Supreme Court announcement, his father alerting the grocery store and the little Sweet Home school so everyone would hear Brown, and how he watched white farmers to acquire broken plows the vocational students rewelded for the family.

title: Take This Quarter Up to the Poor Pit
summary: Jones describes being sent with his brothers to seal and chink the leaky houses of families who would be tossed off the land if they repaired them, at no charge, and his father making a brother return a quarter he had accepted by dropping it in the church offering, because the work was something they were to do for folks.

title: I'm Saving for Hard Time
topic: Pushed at School and Paid Nothing
summary: He recalls learning from older siblings that his parents told teachers to push him hard because he was smart, being handed every school assignment from MC to agriculture leader, his father refusing to pay his own children because he was saving for hard time, and the day his father gave him twenty-five dollars at a district basketball tournament that made other kids call him rich.

title: Go to the Head of the Line
summary: Jones describes how his parents followed Little Rock's Central High closely, and his mother telling him that since the family had already integrated a local bus line by riding in the front seat, he should never be afraid to act for other people, never go to the back of the line, but go to the head and be brave about it.

title: We Weighed Our Own Cotton
summary: He tells how, while white gin operators shorted other Black sharecroppers by tens of pounds per bag, his mother, father, uncle Bluford, and aunt sat at the gin with double-barreled shotguns and shells between their fingers so the Jones family could weigh their own cotton, with the children watching the theft done to everyone else.

title: They Came to Our House to Kill Him
summary: Jones recounts his brother Troy, working a railroad job, refusing to leave the train at a small Arkansas town that barred Negroes, stabbing and cutting white men before being shot and escaping through the woods, and the night a white mob came down the dirt road to the farm only to be shot back and disarmed by his father and relatives.

title: You Look Just Like Ernest Jones
summary: He describes reaching the city of Pine Bluff and its college, carrying a letter from his brother in Milwaukee to a vocational professor who recognized him on sight as Ernest Jones's son, walked him around campus calling him one of his boys, and told the faculty to keep their eyes on the good student from a small-town high school that had taught algebra, trigonometry, physics, and chemistry.

title: Built With Dormitories for the Quakers
summary: Jones explains that his hometown high school, one of two all-Black school districts able to float bonds, was built around 1929 with dormitories by a New York group he thought were Quakers, drew handpicked teachers who lived on campus, and gave students real books rather than white schools' castoffs, an unusually strong start.

title: You've Already Done What We Tried to Do
summary: He recounts his professor housing him and introducing him to organizers trying to start a civil rights effort, and Worth Long and Bill Hansen arriving to tell him that the upbringing he described, integrating buses and refusing back doors, meant he had already done what they had been trying to do, and that the college town's heavy segregation needed organizing.

title: I'm a White Boy and I Don't Need to Be Leading
topic: Why They Wanted a Black Leader
summary: Jones describes Worth Long's labor-union background and Bill Hansen's view that as a white man he should not lead, that they needed a Black student to challenge segregated lunch counters, and Hansen testing whether Jones's parents would object, to which Jones answered that they had raised him on going through front doors and would support him, settling that his mother was already civil rights.

title: They Want to Be Thugs
summary: He tells of recruiting students with the rule that anyone whose parents would object should stay home, and a principal steering him to brave, rough teenagers at Townsend Park High School, whom Jones won over by telling his own story and explaining that this would be a nonviolent group even when they were hit in the face and knocked down.

title: Diane Nash Said It's Open
summary: Jones recounts the first day in February 1963 finding Woolworth locked, then Diane Nash, a very light-skinned veteran of the Freedom Rides, going in and being served as white before telling them it was open, and her strategy of crowding the counter at eleven o'clock and falling toward the white patrons when the beating started.

title: The President Suspended Us
summary: He describes the college president, Dr. Lawrence A. Davis Sr., calling the students in and parents arriving to snatch their children home, leaving only three or four including Jones and a young woman, Joanne, who later became a professor there, with the president declaring anyone who went downtown the next day suspended.

title: They Locked Us In and Turned the Gas On
summary: Jones calls the Magnolia sit-in the most violent, where the owner locked the students inside and turned on a stinging fog gas while an armed crowd waited outside, and how he survived by recalling his brother Troy's advice about water against gas, turning on the sink and getting everyone to put their heads in the water.

title: I'll Bond Everybody Out of Jail
summary: He recounts the Black business community coming down hard, with only two backers: Mr. Brown the funeral home owner, who quietly promised to bond everyone out of jail and whose son agreed to help, and a prominent dentist who offered to fix free of charge anyone hit in the mouth.

title: My Church Door Is Open Day or Night
summary: Jones tells of Reverend B.E. Allen, pastor of a large Methodist church, appearing in a blue suit to vouch for the students as future leaders, his Methodist standing seeming to calm the police, his offer of his church day or night, and the Townsend Park boys agreeing to stay nonviolent through the first arrest.

title: A Real Fear Moment
summary: He describes the first successful restaurant integration in Pine Bluff in March and the looming reality of mass arrests, the college crowd's nervousness against the young toughs who had already done jail time, and the quiet reassurance of seeing supporters like Mr. Coleman watching from across the street.

title: The Old Black People Came Out
summary: Jones recounts the jailings drawing out the older Black population, who packed Reverend B.E. Allen's church with rolls of money to bond the students, brought fried chicken the jailers eventually let them eat, and shifted the city so that white reaction turned to thrown objects and slurs rather than knockdowns, even as a campus rumor that demonstrations would cost the football team its scholarships frightened the players.

title: I'll Handle the Case
topic: A Young Lawyer and the Hotel
summary: He describes the small Black law firm whose lawyer, later a Bill Clinton federal-judge appointee, walked into the church to say he would handle the case before leaving, and returning to Woolworth and a hotel that quietly desegregated by asking that Black guests come after dark to avoid white retaliation.

title: My Wife Never Went Back
summary: Jones recounts that the first suspension wave left three or four who later returned to school, that he never did, and that the woman he married on May 16, 1964 was in the first Woolworth group though not arrested at the hotel, and like seven or eight others never went back to college.

part: Taking the Lead in the Delta
title: They Made Jim Jones the Head
summary: He reflects on how the interracial environment hardened resentment because whites like Bill Hansen were perceived as leading, how the group deliberately had Jones launch mass meetings so Black audiences would respond, how Bill and Ruthie's interracial marriage inflamed Pine Bluff, and how Jones was formally made head of the effort to refocus attention off the white organizers.

title: They Going to Shoot at You
summary: As the project-director title shifted to him in 1964, Jones describes shutting down the aggressive Hansen and choosing voter-registration targets in the hard, conservative plantation country of West Helena, Forrest City, and Marianna, insisting that he and Worth Long go because white reaction there would key on Hansen's skin, and Mrs. Clay of Forrest City opening her house to him.

title: A Direct Connection to the Community
summary: Jones describes traveling to Atlanta SNCC coordinating meetings where the educated big-city organizers showed off with big words and theory while he spoke concretely about the rural people he served, and James Forman telling him his direct connection to the community was a wonderful thing.

title: Put Your Shoes On and Knock on Doors
summary: He recounts asking Atlanta to send a great singing group rather than organizers because his church-folk crowds would love it, getting people to knock on doors for voter registration, and persuading a respected plant manager in Forrest City to run and win a local office, alongside Mrs. Clay's help and the SNCC drive toward the 1966 governor's race.

title: Some Folks Were Stuck
summary: Jones argues that some activists saw only public accommodation and, once restaurants and hotels opened, wanted to chase distant fights like protesting in Chicago, while he pushed the new piece, getting this population access to voting and running candidates, faulting those who could not move past the one idea they had.

title: Go Talk to Mrs. Clay
summary: He describes a voter-registration week in the Delta following advice to find, in nearly every community, the woman who knew the politically active people, going first to the Black barbershop in Forrest City where the men pointed him to Mrs. Clay, who offered him a choice of her houses to stay in and, with her principal sister, packed a church that night to launch a candidate.

title: By Then a Lot More Were Elected
summary: Jones recalls another woman in Forrest City who had run two years earlier without votes and joined him eagerly, her college-educated son, and returning later to find many more Black officials elected and a man he had worked with become an attorney and, by Bill Clinton's appointment, a federal judge, with a health clinic among the gains in East Arkansas.

title: I Don't Get Mad With Folks
summary: Jones reflects on his personality of incredible tolerance, never seeing low turnout as failure, preferring a small high-energy group to seventy-five reluctant people, the way some folks do in church, and crediting his ability to recruit and identify people who then took off and did a lot of things.

title: I See So Many Babies
summary: He tells of a Georgia town gutted by a factory closing, where residents in a meeting dreamed of building department stores and high-rises until Jones pointed out that with so many children no one could go to work, and a prominent schoolteacher revealed her brother, a hustler and gambler, owned the big empty warehouse standing there.

title: Are You Jesus Christ?
summary: Jones describes persuading the gambler brother to give the group his warehouse for a nursery so the mothers could take jobs at a nearby military base hiring heavy labor, and the women tearing out walls and hauling lumber to the street themselves, astonishing the owner and a passing lumberman who asked if Jones was God.

title: Stack Them In Like Sardines
summary: He recounts a lumber company donating used but good wood, the women completing the nursery, and his advice to refuse federal money because square-footage-per-child rules would price out most families, where privately owning it let them keep many children and put non-working mothers to work caring for them, a self-running model he credits to believing folks can do it themselves.

title: It Was Not a Lifetime Situation
summary: Turning to his 1965 departure for Atlanta, Jones describes the SNCC staff in Arkansas of about eight, evenly Black and white, with racial-leadership tensions sharpening amid the hot summer of 1965 and the first riots, and his sense that resources and the people with early energy were drifting toward making a living, while white volunteers wanted a lifetime situation the work could not be.

title: SNCC Went As Far As It Could
summary: He recalls telling Bill Hansen and the men that they had done a good job but were stretching the work thin with no income, that as the Black Panthers and Stokely Carmichael tried to rebuild something new this phase was ending, and that with aging organizers needing jobs and Black officials being elected, SNCC had reached the limit of its capabilities and could not be a lifetime instrument.

title: They Wanted the Top Gun
summary: Asked about Worth Long's challenge to abandoning integration and the rise of Bobby Brown and the Black United in Little Rock, Jones says he heard of it from afar and saw it as a small faction satisfying Brown and Stokely Carmichael, who he believed always wanted to be the top gun and a next Malcolm X for the media rather than focusing on benefits to people.

title: Closer, Fresher, a Lot Cheaper
summary: Working with sharecroppers, Jones describes his assignment identifying small farmers across Virginia, Georgia, Florida, and South Carolina and connecting them to national chain stores eager for closer, fresher, cheaper Black-farmer produce, against others fixated on the big farmers' supposed money and high-volume shipping that overlooked five acres of tomatoes harvested four times and sold to a nearby Kroger.

title: I Always Saw Them As Contacts
summary: Asked whether slow economic progress made him doubt the integrationist model, Jones says he never thought it was the only way, that he used well-connected white volunteers not for their money but as contacts for outlets and college scholarships in the Northeast and Ohio, advising farmers' children that with no one to run the land they should hold property that would later be worth fortunes in condos.

title: Trees of Dollars Falling Out of the Sky
summary: Jones credits his farm background with keeping him grounded against movement people whose theories depended on government action the people were not ready for, and recounts goals that did not happen, recruiting nurses and doctors to small-town hospitals across East Arkansas, while finding satisfaction in the growth of young people who excelled and moved on rather than reading their departure as disappointment.

title: Ninety-Five Percent Were Dead and Gone
topic: On Compensation and a Life's Work
summary: Jones closes by wishing the fifty thousand Black farmers denied loans could have been paid, but noting that by the time recognition came most were dead and their children scattered to Chicago and Detroit, doubting that compensating a grandchild who never knew the farming grandfather honors the hard work, and saying every job kept him close to agriculture and small towns working on behalf of people, the joy of his life, before the interviewer's thanks and the closing tag.
